findings.description: sorted() with nan values produces implementation-dependent ordering (nan comparisons return False in CPython, so nan can land at any position); the returned percentile is silently wrong whenever the input contains nan, with no error raised.

Incremental sliding-window accumulation (current += values[i] - values[i-window]) accumulates floating-point rounding error over long sequences; for large N or large-magnitude values the running sum drifts from the true window sum, producing silently wrong averages.

insort uses bisect which relies on __lt__; since nan < x is always False, nan is inserted at an unpredictable index, permanently breaking the sorted invariant of _data; every subsequent median() call silently returns a wrong value.

safe_mean returns nan (not None) when any element is nan; the Optional[float] return type and 'None for empty input' docstring imply valid-float-or-None, so a nan-contaminated non-empty input silently violates the implied contract.

clamp(float('nan'), low, high) silently returns low because Python's max/min with nan returns the non-nan operand; the caller receives a plausible-looking value with no indication the input was invalid.

Exit-bar condition unverifiable: tests were NOT RUN (fixture corpus, no test suite); the convergence bar requires a full passing test run — this condition cannot be satisfied from static analysis alone.
